params.text:
When someone is still unhappy

Sometimes a person will remain dissatisfied even after the service has responded fully. They have every right to take the matter further. The service should always make clear, at the end of its response, how the person can escalate — never leave them at a dead end.

For adult social care in England, the independent next step is the Local Government and Social Care Ombudsman (LGSCO). The LGSCO is a free, impartial service that investigates complaints about councils and care providers and decides whether they acted fairly.

  • The person should normally complete the service’s own complaints process first.
  • They can go to the LGSCO once they have a final response and are still unhappy — or if the service has not responded within a reasonable time (generally up to around 12 weeks).
  • The LGSCO can recommend remedies such as an apology, financial redress, or changes to improve the service.

Bringing it together

Good complaints handling protects people, improves care and strengthens trust. Welcome complaints as a gift, respond with listening and a sincere apology, follow the process fairly and within agreed timescales, report everything without promising outcomes, learn from what you hear, and be open in the spirit of the duty of candour. When someone remains unhappy, point them to the LGSCO. Do this well, and complaints become one of the most powerful tools you have for delivering safe, person-centred care.

params.question: A person should normally complete the care provider’s own complaints process before taking their complaint to the Local Government and Social Care Ombudsman.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. The person should normally finish the provider’s own complaints process first before escalating to the LGSCO.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. The LGSCO usually expects the provider’s own process to be completed first — though a person can also go to them if there has been no response within a reasonable time.
